nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs and rocky shores; generally found solitary or in small groups on sheltered reef habitats with adjacent sandy patches for sleeping refuge.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open swimming space across the mid-water column
tightly fitting lid or mesh cover (jumper)

diet.notes: In the wild feeds on small invertebrates — polychaetes, copepods, isopods, and foraminifera. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, marine pellets, and quality flake food. Multiple small feedings per day tend to keep activity and color at their best.

avoidWith: other Halichoeres wrasses of similar size
ornamental shrimp (cleaner shrimp, peppermint shrimp, sexy shrimp)
dragonets and mandarins (food competition for small inverts)

summary: Active, beginner-friendly reef wrasse with electric green-blue body and pinstripe markings; sleeps buried in fine sand and helps control common reef pests.
careNotes: The Melanurus Wrasse is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly Halichoeres species for a reef tank — hardy, colorful, and active throughout the day. Has been reported to reach about 11–14 cm; most captive specimens settle around 12–13 cm. Generally advised to provide a tank of at least ~250 L with a footprint that allows open mid-water swimming, since these are tireless cruisers rather than rock-hugging fish. A fine-sand substrate of roughly 5–10 cm is important — at night and when frightened the fish dives in and sleeps buried, and a coarse or shallow bed can injure or stress it. A tightly fitting lid or mesh top is strongly recommended; this species is a known jumper. Considered reef-safe with corals and clams, and is often valued as a working pest controller: has been documented eating pyramidellid snails, flatworms, bristleworms, and certain external parasites. The trade-off is that hungry individuals tend to predate ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy) and may snack on small ornamental snails or feather-duster worms. Like most wrasses it is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin life female and the dominant fish can transition to male — so a tank typically holds only one Melanurus unless the system is large enough to support a harem (~400 L+). Generally peaceful toward unrelated species but can be territorial toward other Halichoeres or similarly shaped wrasses, especially in tighter quarters. Also sold under the trade names 'Tail-Spot Wrasse' (or 'Tailspot Wrasse'), 'Pinstripe Wrasse', and occasionally 'Hoeven's Wrasse'. Source spread for tank size ran from 50 US gal (~190 L) entry-level to 100–125 US gal (~380–475 L) for full-adult housing; 250 L picked as a sensible reef-active midpoint per the tank-size rule.
